verses.text: And it came to pass after many days, that the word of the LORD came to Elijah in the third year, saying, Go, shew thyself unto Ahab; and I will send rain upon the earth. And Elijah went to shew himself unto Ahab. And there was a sore famine in Samaria. Guidance: The third year. The drought has been crushing the land for three years (James 5:17 specifies three and a half). The famine in Samaria has reached the desperate stage. Now the LORD speaks: 'Go, shew thyself unto Ahab; and I will send rain upon the earth.' The two halves of the command are connected: Elijah's reappearance and the rain's return are paired. The prophet who declared the drought must publicly stand again before the king who has been hunting him; only then will the LORD release the rain. The chapter is setting up the choreography. The whole drought has been a controlled demonstration: the rain stopped at Elijah's word (1 Kgs 17:1) and will return at the LORD's appointed moment, which requires Elijah's reappearance. Baal — the storm-god supposedly responsible for rain — has not been able to produce a single drop for three years. The LORD will now make absolutely clear, in a public contest, which God really gives rain. The chapter is showing us the LORD's patience with His own discipline. He waits 'many days' before relieving the famine, allowing the drought to bring Israel to the point where the public will actually choose. The chapter is teaching us that divine discipline often requires extended duration to produce repentance; momentary discomfort can be brushed aside, but years of famine bring people to ask the deeper question: which God actually controls reality?

And Ahab called Obadiah, which was the governor of his house. (Now Obadiah feared the LORD greatly: For it was so, when Jezebel cut off the prophets of the LORD, that Obadiah took an hundred prophets, and hid them by fifty in a cave, and fed them with bread and water.) And Ahab said unto Obadiah, Go into the land, unto all fountains of water, and unto all brooks: peradventure we may find grass to save the horses and mules alive, that we lose not all the beasts. So they divided the land between them to pass throughout it: Ahab went one way by himself, and Obadiah went another way by himself. Guidance: Obadiah — a quiet hero of the chapter. The narrator introduces him with care. He is 'the governor of [Ahab's] house' (al ha-bayit — palace steward, equivalent to chief of staff). He holds the most senior administrative position in the king's household. The parenthetical reveals his secret life: 'Obadiah feared the LORD greatly.' Hebrew yare et-YHWH me'od. The man closest to Ahab's daily operations is, in fact, a devout believer in the LORD. The chapter is showing us a paradox of court life: a faithful man can serve a wicked regime, hold senior office, and use his position for the LORD's purposes. (Compare Daniel in Babylon, Esther in Persia, Joseph in Egypt.) Obadiah's specific service: 'when Jezebel cut off the prophets of the LORD' — Jezebel had launched a systematic purge of the LORD's prophets, ordering them killed — 'Obadiah took an hundred prophets, and hid them by fifty in a cave, and fed them with bread and water.' At enormous personal risk, Obadiah used his palace resources to save 100 prophetic lives, hiding them in two batches of 50 in caves (probably in the Carmel range), feeding them through the famine. The chapter is preserving the existence of this hidden faithful remnant. Elijah will say in v22 that he alone remains; Obadiah's 100 hidden men reveal that the remnant is larger than Elijah knows. Even in apostate Israel, the LORD has His seven thousand (1 Kgs 19:18); Obadiah was feeding 100 of them in caves. The chapter is teaching us that faithfulness in evil times often operates in hidden ways. Obadiah did not publicly confront Ahab or Jezebel; he could not have survived if he had. But he used his position to preserve those who could not preserve themselves. The chapter is showing us that the LORD has many kinds of servants: the public prophet who declares drought, and the hidden steward who feeds the underground prophets. Both are needed; both are honored.

And as Obadiah was in the way, behold, Elijah met him: and he knew him, and fell on his face, and said, Art thou that my lord Elijah? And he answered him, I am: go, tell thy lord, Behold, Elijah is here. And he said, What have I sinned, that thou wouldest deliver thy servant into the hand of Ahab, to slay me? As the LORD thy God liveth, there is no nation or kingdom, whither my lord hath not sent to seek thee: and when they said, He is not there; he took an oath of the kingdom and nation, that they found thee not. And now thou sayest, Go, tell thy lord, Behold, Elijah is here. And it shall come to pass, as soon as I am gone from thee, that the Spirit of the LORD shall carry thee whither I know not; and so when I come and tell Ahab, and he cannot find thee, he shall slay me: but I thy servant fear the LORD from my youth. Was it not told my lord what I did when Jezebel slew the prophets of the LORD, how I hid an hundred men of the LORD's prophets by fifty in a cave, and fed them with bread and water? And now thou sayest, Go, tell thy lord, Behold, Elijah is here: and he shall slay me. And Elijah said, As the LORD of hosts liveth, before whom I stand, I will surely shew myself unto him to day. Guidance: Obadiah meets Elijah. The encounter is dramatic. Obadiah, searching for grass, sees the famous prophet on the road; he recognizes him instantly. 'He knew him, and fell on his face, and said, Art thou that my lord Elijah?' The bow signals respect; the question signals shock — Elijah, missing for three years, the most wanted man in the kingdom, suddenly here in this remote pasture. Elijah's immediate instruction: 'Go, tell thy lord, Behold, Elijah is here.' He wants Ahab brought to him. Obadiah's response unfolds at length, in three movements. (1) The fear: 'What have I sinned, that thou wouldest deliver thy servant into the hand of Ahab, to slay me?' Obadiah anticipates a trap. If he tells Ahab Elijah is here and then the prophet vanishes (which Elijah has done before — 'the Spirit of the LORD shall carry thee whither I know not'), Ahab will have Obadiah killed for false report. (2) The proof of his diligence: 'There is no nation or kingdom, whither my lord [Ahab] hath not sent to seek thee.' Ahab has been searching the world for Elijah for three years, demanding sworn affidavits from foreign rulers ('he took an oath of the kingdom and nation, that they found thee not'). Obadiah is telling Elijah that the political stakes of his location-announcement are enormous; Ahab has invested years of diplomatic effort. (3) The plea for his life: 'But I thy servant fear the LORD from my youth.' Obadiah names his lifelong faith. 'Was it not told my lord what I did when Jezebel slew the prophets of the LORD?' He reminds Elijah of his cave-feeding ministry — proof that he is on the LORD's side, even within Ahab's court. The chapter is showing us a faithful man's honest fear. Obadiah is not refusing to obey; he is asking Elijah to understand what is being asked of him. The man who has risked his life to save 100 prophets is now being asked to risk it again, and he is candid about the cost. Elijah's reply is the chapter's most powerful oath-formula: 'As the LORD of hosts liveth, before whom I stand, I will surely shew myself unto him to day.' Hebrew chai-YHWH tzeva'ot asher amadti lefanav. The LORD of hosts (tzeva'ot — the LORD of the armies of heaven) — a name that emphasizes divine military power. Elijah is not just claiming to stand before God; he is claiming to stand before the commander of heaven's armies, and that commander's word is reliable. He will appear today; Obadiah will not be set up. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's call sometimes places His faithful servants in agonizing positions where the cost of obedience seems unbearable. Obadiah's fear was rational; Elijah's promise was reliable. Both are honestly recorded.

So Obadiah went to meet Ahab, and told him: and Ahab went to meet Elijah. And it came to pass, when Ahab saw Elijah, that Ahab said unto him, Art thou he that troubleth Israel? And he answered, I have not troubled Israel; but thou, and thy father's house, in that ye have forsaken the commandments of the LORD, and thou hast followed Baalim. Guidance: The first confrontation. Obadiah obeys, finds Ahab, delivers the message; Ahab comes to meet Elijah. The king's opening line is accusatory: 'Art thou he that troubleth Israel?' Hebrew ha-atta zeh ocher Yisrael — 'are you he, troubler of Israel?' The verb akhar means to bring trouble, to throw into confusion, to disturb. Ahab is laying the responsibility for the three-year drought on Elijah — the prophet's word stopped the rain, so the prophet is the troubler. Elijah's response is the chapter's first thunderclap. 'I have not troubled Israel; but thou, and thy father's house, in that ye have forsaken the commandments of the LORD, and thou hast followed Baalim.' The Hebrew is precise: ki ata u-beit avikha ba-azevkhem et-mitzvot YHWH — 'in that you have abandoned the LORD's commandments.' Elijah reverses the accusation completely. The drought is not the prophet's fault; it is the king's. Ahab and his dynastic line (Omri and his court) have abandoned the LORD and chased after Baalim — the plural form, indicating multiple local Baal-manifestations worshiped across Israel. Elijah's identification of cause is correct deuteronomic theology. Deut 11:16-17 had explicitly warned: 'Take heed to yourselves, that your heart be not deceived, and ye turn aside, and serve other gods, and worship them; and then the LORD's wrath be kindled against you, and he shut up the heaven, that there be no rain.' The drought was the precise consequence promised in the law for exactly the sin Ahab has committed. The chapter is teaching us that the diagnosis of national troubles often lies not in the symptom (Elijah's word stopping rain) but in the deeper cause (the kingdom's apostasy). Ahab blames the messenger; Elijah names the disease. The chapter is preserving one of the great prophetic truths: when religion fails, society's leaders typically blame the prophets who name the failure rather than the actions that caused it.

Now therefore send, and gather to me all Israel unto mount Carmel, and the prophets of Baal four hundred and fifty, and the prophets of the groves four hundred, which eat at Jezebel's table. So Ahab sent unto all the children of Israel, and gathered the prophets together unto mount Carmel. And Elijah came unto all the people, and said, How long halt ye between two opinions? if the LORD be God, follow him: but if Baal, then follow him. And the people answered him not a word. Guidance: The Carmel summons and the famous challenge. Elijah's commands. (1) 'Gather to me all Israel.' Israel here means all of Ahab's subjects across the northern kingdom — a national assembly. (2) 'The prophets of Baal four hundred and fifty.' The professional Baal-priesthood, Jezebel's imported Phoenician clergy. (3) 'The prophets of the groves four hundred' — the Asherah-prophets, the female-goddess clergy who worship Baal's consort. 850 prophets of foreign deities total, all on Jezebel's royal payroll ('eat at Jezebel's table'). Mount Carmel — the prominent coastal ridge that runs from the sea inland — was chosen for symbolic reasons. It bordered Phoenician and Israelite territory (a contested area where Baal-worship was particularly entrenched), it was visible from a great distance, and it had a long history as a sacred site. Ahab complies (probably because his curiosity about ending the drought outweighs his fear of Elijah, and because he assumes 850 Baal-prophets cannot lose to one Elijah). When the people gather, Elijah delivers the chapter's most famous line. 'How long halt ye between two opinions? if the LORD be God, follow him: but if Baal, then follow him.' The Hebrew ad-matai attem poschim al-shtei ha-se'ipim — 'how long are you limping on two crutches/branches?' The verb pasach is the same word used for the Passover (Pesach — when the LORD passed over the houses) but here in a different sense: limping, hopping back and forth. Elijah's image is of a man trying to walk by leaping between two supports, never committing to either. The people are trying to worship both the LORD and Baal — keeping their Israelite identity while accepting the royal Phoenician cult. They want both. Elijah demands a choice. 'And the people answered him not a word.' Hebrew ve-lo-anu ha-am oto davar — silence. The people will not answer because they have no defensible answer. They cannot say 'we follow the LORD' because their behavior contradicts the claim; they cannot say 'we follow Baal' because their conscience refuses. They are caught in the silence of half-commitment that Elijah has just named. The chapter is teaching us a profound principle of religious life. The greatest danger is not outright apostasy (which is at least decisive) but unwillingness to choose. Trying to combine the LORD with Baal produces nothing — neither the security of true worship nor the supposed benefits of false. The people's silence is the chapter's first verdict on syncretism: it leaves you with nothing to say.

The verb pasach is the same word used for the Passover (Pesach — when the LORD passed over the houses) but here in a different sense: limping, hopping back and forth. Elijah's image is of a man trying to walk by leaping between two supports, never committing to either. The people are trying to worship both the LORD and Baal — keeping their Israelite identity while accepting the royal Phoenician cult. They want both. Elijah demands a choice. 'And the people answered him not a word.' Hebrew ve-lo-anu ha-am oto davar — silence. The people will not answer because they have no defensible answer. They cannot say 'we follow the LORD' because their behavior contradicts the claim; they cannot say 'we follow Baal' because their conscience refuses. They are caught in the silence of half-commitment that Elijah has just named. The chapter is teaching us a profound principle of religious life. The greatest danger is not outright apostasy (which is at least decisive) but unwillingness to choose. Trying to combine the LORD with Baal produces nothing — neither the security of true worship nor the supposed benefits of false. The people's silence is the chapter's first verdict on syncretism: it leaves you with nothing to say.

Then said Elijah unto the people, I, even I only, remain a prophet of the LORD; but Baal's prophets are four hundred and fifty men. Let them therefore give us two bullocks; and let them choose one bullock for themselves, and cut it in pieces, and lay it on wood, and put no fire under: and I will dress the other bullock, and lay it on wood, and put no fire under: And call ye on the name of your gods, and I will call on the name of the LORD: and the God that answereth by fire, let him be God. And all the people answered and said, It is well spoken. Guidance: The contest proposed. Elijah names his solitude: 'I, even I only, remain a prophet of the LORD.' He knows Obadiah's 100 hidden prophets exist somewhere in caves, but they are not on Carmel; he stands alone before the assembly. 'Baal's prophets are four hundred and fifty men.' The numerical asymmetry is staggering — 1 versus 450 (the Asherah-prophets of v19 may or may not have shown up; the rest of the chapter focuses on the 450 Baal-prophets). The terms of the contest. (1) Two bullocks, one for each side. (2) Each side prepares its own sacrifice, places it on wood, but lights no fire. (3) Each side calls on its god. (4) The god who answers by fire is God. Elijah's contest design is theologically masterful. Fire from heaven was a sign of divine acceptance of sacrifice (Lev 9:24, the inauguration of the Aaronic priesthood; 1 Chr 21:26, David's altar at Araunah's threshing floor). The visible, undeniable arrival of fire would settle the question. Even more, Baal was supposedly the storm-god who controlled lightning — fire from the sky was precisely his claimed power. If Baal could not deliver lightning on his own altar, his core deity-claim would be exposed as nothing. The people respond: 'It is well spoken.' Hebrew tov ha-davar — 'good is the word.' The contest is fair; the test is clean; both sides agree to the terms. The chapter is showing us a contest that the Baal-prophets must accept because refusing would already be defeat. They cannot decline a public test of the deity they claim to serve; their refusal would prove they doubted their own god. They must engage. They will lose. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's challenges to false worship often take this form — a publicly verifiable test on the terms the false god would claim to be best at. The LORD does not pick artificial contests; He picks the very tests the false gods supposedly excel at, and demonstrates the false gods' inability to perform.

And Elijah said unto the prophets of Baal, Choose you one bullock for yourselves, and dress it first; for ye are many; and call on the name of your gods, but put no fire under. And they took the bullock which was given them, and they dressed it, and called on the name of Baal from morning even until noon, saying, O Baal, hear us. But there was no voice, nor any that answered. And they leaped upon the altar which was made. Guidance: The Baal-prophets' failure. Elijah generously gives them first choice and first effort: 'Choose you one bullock for yourselves, and dress it first; for ye are many.' He is making the contest as favorable to them as possible. They have numerical superiority, choice of victim, first turn, and the entire morning to work with. They prepare the sacrifice and begin calling on Baal. 'From morning even until noon' — five or six hours of intensive ritual invocation. 'O Baal, hear us.' Hebrew habba'al aneinu — 'O Baal, answer us.' The cry is direct, repeated, increasingly urgent. The result: 'There was no voice, nor any that answered.' Hebrew ein qol va-ein oneh — 'no voice and no answerer.' Two emphatic negatives. The silence is total. Baal does not speak. Baal does not respond. The 450 prophets, joined together, calling on their god, achieve no detectable result. 'And they leaped upon the altar which was made.' Hebrew vayephassechu al ha-mizbeach — they limped on the altar. The same verb pasach that Elijah had used in v21 ('how long halt ye') is now used for the Baal-prophets' frantic ritual dancing. The translation captures the irony: Elijah had accused the people of limping between two opinions; the Baal-prophets are now literally limping in their ritual dance around an unresponsive altar. The chapter is showing us religious enthusiasm without divine response. The Baal-prophets are sincere, persistent, professionally trained, numerically overwhelming — and completely impotent. The god they served could not respond because the god they served did not exist as a personal power. The chapter is teaching us that intensity of religious effort cannot conjure a response from a god who is not there. No amount of ritual, repetition, or fervor can compensate for the basic absence of the deity invoked. The Baal-prophets' performance was impeccable by their own standards; it produced nothing because there was nothing to respond.

And it came to pass at noon, that Elijah mocked them, and said, Cry aloud: for he is a god; either he is talking, or he is pursuing, or he is in a journey, or peradventure he sleepeth, and must be awaked. And they cried aloud, and cut themselves after their manner with knives and lancets, till the blood gushed out upon them. And it came to pass, when midday was past, and they prophesied until the time of the offering of the evening sacrifice, that there was neither voice, nor any to answer, nor any that regarded. Guidance: Elijah's mockery and the Baal-prophets' descent into self-mutilation. At noon, Elijah begins to taunt. 'Cry aloud: for he is a god.' Hebrew qiru-be-qol gadol ki-elohim hu — 'call with a loud voice, for he is a god.' The sarcasm is biting: maybe the gods are hard of hearing. Then Elijah offers possible explanations for Baal's silence, all deliberately ridiculous. (1) 'Either he is talking' — Baal is in conversation, can't be interrupted. (2) 'Or he is pursuing' — Baal is busy with other business. The Hebrew sig might also be translated 'has gone aside' — i.e., relieving himself, a more vulgar mockery. (3) 'Or he is in a journey' — Baal is traveling and out of range. (4) 'Or peradventure he sleepeth, and must be awaked' — perhaps Baal is napping. Each suggestion is calculated to humiliate. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's servants can be openly mocking toward false gods. Elijah does not engage in polite interfaith dialogue; he treats Baal-worship with the contempt he believes it deserves. The chapter is recording one of the Old Testament's most savage prophetic put-downs. The Baal-prophets respond by escalating. 'They cried aloud, and cut themselves after their manner with knives and lancets, till the blood gushed out upon them.' Self-cutting was a standard Phoenician-Canaanite practice — believed to attract the gods' attention through the worshiper's pain and bloodshed. (Compare 1 Kgs 18:28 in the deuteronomic context with Deut 14:1, which forbade Israelites to cut themselves for the dead.) The prophets' bleeding became extreme. Then prophesying — a frenzied ecstatic state, accompanied by trances and visions — continued until the late afternoon. The result remained the same: 'There was neither voice, nor any to answer, nor any that regarded.' Three emphatic negatives now (instead of two earlier). The chapter is showing us the descent of false worship into self-harm. When the god does not respond, the worshipers escalate their physical sacrifice — their own bodies — hoping to compel a response. Nothing comes. The chapter is teaching us that false religion produces self-destruction. A god who is not there cannot be reached by any amount of suffering offered to him; the suffering goes to nothing, into the void, and only leaves the worshipers bleeding for nothing.

And Elijah said unto all the people, Come near unto me. And all the people came near unto him. And he repaired the altar of the LORD that was broken down. And Elijah took twelve stones, according to the number of the tribes of the sons of Jacob, unto whom the word of the LORD came, saying, Israel shall be thy name: And with the stones he built an altar in the name of the LORD: and he made a trench about the altar, as great as would contain two measures of seed. And he put the wood in order, and cut the bullock in pieces, and laid him on the wood, and said, Fill four barrels with water, and pour it on the burnt sacrifice, and on the wood. And he said, Do it the second time. And they did it the second time. And he said, Do it the third time. And they did it the third time. And the water ran round about the altar; and he filled the trench also with water. Guidance: Elijah's preparation. After the Baal-prophets' day-long failure, Elijah takes his turn. (1) 'Come near unto me.' He calls the people in, making them eyewitnesses. (2) The altar. 'He repaired the altar of the LORD that was broken down' — an older LORD-altar on Carmel had stood at some earlier date (possibly from the period of the judges) but had been demolished, presumably during Jezebel's persecution. Elijah rebuilds it. (3) The twelve stones. 'According to the number of the tribes of the sons of Jacob.' This is a deliberate theological statement. The northern kingdom of Ahab includes only ten of the original twelve tribes; the other two (Judah and Benjamin) are in the south. By using twelve stones, Elijah is asserting that the LORD's covenant is with all Israel, not just the politically truncated north. The kingdom-division has not erased the covenant. (4) The trench. 'As great as would contain two measures of seed' — about two seahs, perhaps 2 gallons of capacity. The trench encircles the altar. (5) The wood and the bullock. Standard sacrificial preparation. (6) The water. 'Fill four barrels with water, and pour it on the burnt sacrifice, and on the wood.' Done. 'Do it the second time.' Done. 'Do it the third time.' Done. Twelve barrels of water (the same number as the twelve stones) drench the wood, the sacrifice, and overflow into the trench. In a drought-stricken land, twelve barrels of water was an extravagant expenditure — Elijah was using up scarce resources to ensure that no one could later claim some hidden combustion source. The wet altar, wet wood, wet meat, and full trench made fire from natural causes impossible. The chapter is showing us prophetic theater. Elijah is staging the contest so that the LORD's victory will be unambiguous. The Baal-prophets had a dry altar and dry wood and still got no fire. Elijah has a soaked altar, soaked wood, and a full trench, and is asking the LORD to deliver fire anyway. The chapter is teaching us a principle: when defending the LORD's honor publicly, do not avoid the hardest scenario. Make the test maximally clear. The LORD does not need the contest tilted in His favor; let the impossibility of the situation magnify His victory when it comes.

(1) 'Come near unto me.' He calls the people in, making them eyewitnesses. (2) The altar. 'He repaired the altar of the LORD that was broken down' — an older LORD-altar on Carmel had stood at some earlier date (possibly from the period of the judges) but had been demolished, presumably during Jezebel's persecution. Elijah rebuilds it. (3) The twelve stones. 'According to the number of the tribes of the sons of Jacob.' This is a deliberate theological statement. The northern kingdom of Ahab includes only ten of the original twelve tribes; the other two (Judah and Benjamin) are in the south. By using twelve stones, Elijah is asserting that the LORD's covenant is with all Israel, not just the politically truncated north. The kingdom-division has not erased the covenant. (4) The trench. 'As great as would contain two measures of seed' — about two seahs, perhaps 2 gallons of capacity. The trench encircles the altar. (5) The wood and the bullock. Standard sacrificial preparation. (6) The water. 'Fill four barrels with water, and pour it on the burnt sacrifice, and on the wood.' Done. 'Do it the second time.' Done. 'Do it the third time.' Done. Twelve barrels of water (the same number as the twelve stones) drench the wood, the sacrifice, and overflow into the trench. In a drought-stricken land, twelve barrels of water was an extravagant expenditure — Elijah was using up scarce resources to ensure that no one could later claim some hidden combustion source. The wet altar, wet wood, wet meat, and full trench made fire from natural causes impossible. The chapter is showing us prophetic theater. Elijah is staging the contest so that the LORD's victory will be unambiguous. The Baal-prophets had a dry altar and dry wood and still got no fire. Elijah has a soaked altar, soaked wood, and a full trench, and is asking the LORD to deliver fire anyway. The chapter is teaching us a principle: when defending the LORD's honor publicly, do not avoid the hardest scenario. Make the test maximally clear. The LORD does not need the contest tilted in His favor; let the impossibility of the situation magnify His victory when it comes.

And it came to pass at the time of the offering of the evening sacrifice, that Elijah the prophet came near, and said, LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel, let it be known this day that thou art God in Israel, and that I am thy servant, and that I have done all these things at thy word. Hear me, O LORD, hear me, that this people may know that thou art the LORD God, and that thou hast turned their heart back again. Then the fire of the LORD fell, and consumed the burnt sacrifice, and the wood, and the stones, and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench. And when all the people saw it, they fell on their faces: and they said, The LORD, he is the God; the LORD, he is the God. Guidance: The fire. The chapter's climax in three movements. (1) The prayer. Brief, deliberate, theologically loaded. 'At the time of the offering of the evening sacrifice' — the traditional time of evening Temple worship in Jerusalem (around 3 PM). Elijah is praying at the moment the faithful priests in Jerusalem are also offering. The unity of worship across the political division is emphasized. The prayer addresses 'LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel.' Note 'Israel' rather than 'Jacob' — Elijah uses the covenant-name that was given to Jacob at Peniel. The God of the patriarchs is being invoked to vindicate the people who bear that name. The prayer's three purposes: 'Let it be known this day that thou art God in Israel' (vindication of the LORD's deity over Baal); 'I am thy servant' (vindication of Elijah's prophetic standing); 'I have done all these things at thy word' (vindication that Elijah's actions were divinely directed, not personal initiative). 'Hear me, O LORD, hear me' — the doubled cry signaling urgency. 'That this people may know that thou art the LORD God, and that thou hast turned their heart back again.' The hoped-for outcome: not Elijah's vindication primarily, but the people's restored hearts. The Hebrew hasibbota et-libbam achoranit — 'thou hast turned their heart back again.' Elijah trusts that the LORD has already begun this turning; the fire would confirm what the LORD was already doing. (2) The fire. 'Then the fire of the LORD fell, and consumed the burnt sacrifice, and the wood, and the stones, and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench.' Hebrew vatippol esh-YHWH va-tochal — 'the LORD's fire fell and ate.' Five things consumed in sequence: the meat, the wood, the stones, the dust, the water. The fire is not normal flame; it consumes inorganic matter (stones, dust) and inverts water's nature (a liquid that normally extinguishes fire is here licked up by fire). The phenomenon is supernatural and undeniable. (3) The people's confession. 'They fell on their faces: and they said, The LORD, he is the God; the LORD, he is the God.' Hebrew YHWH hu ha-Elohim YHWH hu ha-Elohim — the same phrase repeated twice for emphasis. The phrase, in Hebrew, is the source of Elijah's name: Eliyahu — 'my God is YHWH.' What had been one prophet's name now becomes the people's national confession. The chapter is recording one of the most public divine vindications in the entire Bible. The fire settles every question: which God exists, which God responds, which God is the LORD of all the elements. The people who had been silent at v21 now confess publicly. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD can answer prayers at precisely the right dramatic moment with effects so unambiguous that even apostate communities are forced to confess. Carmel becomes the model of evangelistic moments — not by argument, but by the LORD acting in such a way that no other explanation is possible.

And Elijah said unto them, Take the prophets of Baal; let not one of them escape. And they took them: and Elijah brought them down to the brook Kishon, and slew them there. Guidance: The execution at Kishon. Elijah orders the execution of all 450 Baal-prophets. The crowd that just confessed the LORD seizes them and brings them down the mountain to the Kishon brook (a wadi running along the foot of Carmel, dry for most of the year except in flood). Elijah personally executes them ('Elijah brought them down to the brook Kishon, and slew them there'). The act is shocking to modern readers and was severe even by ancient standards. Several factors must be held together. (1) The Mosaic-law context. Deut 13:1-5 had prescribed death for any prophet who urged Israelites to follow other gods. Deut 17:2-7 had prescribed death for those who actively served other gods within Israel. The Baal-prophets had been doing both for years; they were, under Mosaic law, capital offenders many times over. (2) The political-religious situation. As long as the Baal-prophets survived, the cult would continue. Jezebel had personally exterminated the LORD's prophets (1 Kgs 18:4); Elijah is now executing her prophets. The judgment is proportional. (3) The covenantal framework. Israel was a theocracy under the Mosaic covenant. Religious authority and civil authority were not separated; foreign-god prophets within Israel were a constitutional crime, not just a private religious preference. (4) The chapter's theological logic. Without removing the entrenched leadership of Baal-worship, the people's momentary confession at the altar would not last. Jezebel would simply restart the cult. Elijah is taking advantage of the moment of national repentance to dismantle the institutional infrastructure of Baalism. The chapter is recording an act of religious violence that fits the deuteronomic theocracy of ancient Israel but does not generalize across all times and places. The chapter is not licensing modern religious persecution; it is recording a specific covenantal moment in which the LORD's people removed the imported foreign cult that had been killing their own prophets. Christians have generally understood this chapter as a unique moment in salvation history, not as an ongoing pattern. The chapter is teaching us, in its own ancient context, that confessing the LORD must be matched by removing what competes with Him. Repentance that leaves the structures of idolatry intact is not lasting repentance.

And Elijah said unto Ahab, Get thee up, eat and drink; for there is a sound of abundance of rain. So Ahab went up to eat and to drink. And Elijah went up to the top of Carmel; and he cast himself down upon the earth, and put his face between his knees, And said to his servant, Go up now, look toward the sea. And he went up, and looked, and said, There is nothing. And he said, Go again seven times. And it came to pass at the seventh time, that he said, Behold, there ariseth a little cloud out of the sea, like a man's hand. And he said, Go up, say unto Ahab, Prepare thy chariot, and get thee down, that the rain stop thee not. And it came to pass in the mean while, that the heaven was black with clouds and wind, and there was a great rain. And Ahab rode, and went to Jezreel. And the hand of the LORD was on Elijah; and he girded up his loins, and ran before Ahab to the entrance of Jezreel. Guidance: The rain returns. With the Baal-prophets dead and the LORD vindicated, the next phase of the prophecy can now unfold — the drought ends. (1) Elijah tells Ahab: 'Get thee up, eat and drink; for there is a sound of abundance of rain.' The king has not eaten in the contest's tension; now he is sent to refresh himself. Elijah hears something Ahab cannot yet hear — the sound of approaching rain. (Hebrew qol hamon ha-geshem — 'the sound of the noise of the rain'; perhaps a prophetic hearing of what is coming.) (2) Elijah's posture. He climbs to Carmel's summit and 'cast himself down upon the earth, and put his face between his knees.' The position is one of intense intercessory prayer — folded onto himself, in the dust, completely humbled. The prophet who has just commanded fire from heaven is now bowed low, asking for rain. (3) The sevenfold watch. 'Go up now, look toward the sea.' The Mediterranean is visible from Carmel's western face. The servant looks: nothing. Six more times he is sent back to look. On the seventh time: 'Behold, there ariseth a little cloud out of the sea, like a man's hand.' Hebrew kekkaf-ish — like the palm of a man. A tiny cloud, the size of a hand held up at arm's length, on the western horizon. (4) The race. Elijah recognizes immediately what the small cloud means: the entire weather system is about to shift, and Jezreel (Ahab's other royal residence, about 17 miles east of Carmel in the valley) must be reached before the road becomes impassable mud. He sends word to Ahab to drive his chariot east. (5) The downpour. 'The heaven was black with clouds and wind, and there was a great rain.' Three and a half years of drought end in hours. The Mediterranean storm system that had been suppressed pours back over the land. The LORD who controls weather has demonstrated control by both stopping and starting. (6) The runner. 'The hand of the LORD was on Elijah; and he girded up his loins, and ran before Ahab to the entrance of Jezreel.' Hebrew vayyad-YHWH hayetah el-Eliyahu — the LORD's hand was on Elijah. Supernaturally empowered, the prophet outruns the king's chariot — about 17 miles, on foot, ahead of horses, in a downpour. The chapter is closing with one of its most stunning images. The same prophet who stood alone against 450 Baal-prophets in the morning, who orchestrated the fire from heaven in the afternoon, who executed the false prophets at evening, now runs ahead of the king's chariot at night through the pouring rain. The LORD's power upon him is total. The chapter is teaching us a beautiful arc. Drought begins by prophet's word; drought ends by prophet's prayer. The fire confirms the contest; the rain confirms the LORD's mercy. The prophet who declared judgment is also the prophet who intercedes for restoration. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's servants are channels for both His discipline and His grace; the same Elijah is the agent of three years of judgment and the agent of restored rain. Christians have often noted: the prophet who calls down fire on enemies is the same prophet who prays for the widow's son's life. Both faces of divine action — judgment and mercy — operate through the same servant. The chapter ends with the prophet running through the rain ahead of the king, the kingdom physically restored to fruitfulness, the people freshly confessing the LORD as God. The narrative high point of Elijah's ministry has been reached. The next chapter will, characteristically, show the human cost of that high point in the prophet's own collapse.
